items.title: JTLIND delivered strong Q4 recovery with 47.6% revenue growth and 104.6% PAT growth, but full-year FY26 performance was mixed - narrowly missed volume...
items.description_html:
JTLIND delivered strong Q4 recovery with 47.6% revenue growth and 104.6% PAT growth, but full-year FY26 performance was mixed - narrowly missed volume and EBITDA/ton guidance.

The company is in aggressive capex mode with 3.2x debt increase, and while operating leverage improved margins, cash conversion remains weak with negative CFO. FY27 guidance sets ambitious 64% volume growth target to 6.5 lakh tons.

Positive Factors

Q4 revenue grew 47.55% YoY to ₹69,268 Lakhs with record volume of 1,23,262 MT

Q4 PAT grew 104.61% YoY to ₹3,441 Lakhs, EBITDA margin expanded 454 bps to 8.34%

Operating leverage visible - expenses grew 41.4% vs revenue growth 47.6%

EBITDA/ton recovered from ₹2,323 in Q1 to ₹4,688 in Q4

Subsidiaries contributed 15.82% of revenue and 10.76% of PAT in FY26

New acquisitions (JTL Defence, Powersol Metalcraft) provide growth diversification

Risk Factors

Negative operating cash flow of ₹6,737 Lakhs (CFO/PAT ratio -0.68x)

Total debt increased 3.2x to ₹244 Cr, debt-to-equity now 0.16x from ~0.06x

Missed FY26 volume guidance (3.96L MT vs 4-4.5L MT target)

Missed EBITDA/ton guidance (₹3,900 vs ₹4,000 target)

Finance costs up 147.9% YoY, interest rate risk materializing

Provisional Attachment Order from Income Tax Department on property

items.title: PVR INOX reported a significant turnaround in Q4 FY26 with revenue growing 25.8% YoY and the core Exhibition segment swinging to profit. The company a...
items.description_html:
PVR INOX reported a significant turnaround in Q4 FY26 with revenue growing 25.8% YoY and the core Exhibition segment swinging to profit. The company achieved major deleveraging with net debt falling 82% to ₹1,703 million and generated strong FCF of ₹19,016 million.

Capex came in significantly below guidance (₹2,587 vs ₹4,000-4,250 million target), demonstrating disciplined capital allocation. Near-term outlook remains positive as balance sheet strengthens toward net-debt-free status.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 25.8% YoY to ₹15,473 million in Q4 FY26
Core Movie Exhibition segment turned profitable at ₹218 million vs loss of ₹1,683 million YoY

Net debt reduced 82.4% YoY to ₹1,703 million, nearing debt-free status

Free cash flow generation of ₹19,016 million in FY26, up 16.2% YoY

EBITDA margin expanded 570 bps YoY to 29.2%

Capex of ₹2,587 million beat guidance of ₹4,000-4,250 million

Total borrowings reduced 49.1% YoY to ₹7,586 million

Risk Factors

Movie Production & Distribution segment revenue declined 7.8% YoY and EBIT fell 43.1% YoY

Screen additions of 79 missed the target of 100-110 screens due to real estate delays

Q4 revenue declined 16.3% QoQ from ₹18,497 million to ₹15,473 million, counter-seasonal

Movie exhibition cost grew 39% YoY, faster than revenue, due to higher distributor shares

items.title: Canara Bank delivered strong guidance beats on asset quality (GNPA 1.84% vs <2.50% target, NNPA 0.43% vs <0.60% target) and growth (advances 16.29% vs...
items.description_html:
Canara Bank delivered strong guidance beats on asset quality (GNPA 1.84% vs <2.50% target, NNPA 0.43% vs <0.60% target) and growth (advances 16.29% vs 10-11% target), with capital adequacy and provisioning remaining robust.

However, the bank missed NIM and CASA guidance while reporting weak NII growth of just 0.4% YoY for FY26, and Q4 PAT declined 9.9% YoY. The execution was good with 8 out of 11 measurable guidance parameters beaten, but margin pressure and funding cost concerns persist.

Positive Factors

Advances growth of 16.29% YoY beat guidance of 10-11% by over 500 bps

Gross NPA improved to 1.84% from 2.94% YoY (110 bps improvement), Net NPA to 0.43% from 0.70% (27 bps)

Provision Coverage Ratio strengthened to 94.21% from 92.70%, well above 93% target

ROE of 20.40% beat guidance of >18.50%, ROA stable at 1.10%

EPS of ₹21.15 beat guidance of >₹19.00

Capital adequacy robust with CRAR at 17.04% and CET1 at 12.44%

Risk Factors

NII grew only 0.4% YoY for full year FY26, indicating severe margin pressure

Q4 PAT declined 9.9% YoY to ₹4,505.57 crore

Other Income declined 24% YoY in Q4 to ₹4,824.36 crore

Cost-to-Income ratio deteriorated to 53.82% in Q4 from 47.55% YoY

CASA ratio target of 32% was missed, deposit growth lagged advances significantly

CFO/PAT ratio very weak at 0.35x indicating profit not translating to cash

items.title: SRGHFL delivered strong Q4 FY26 results with AUM crossing the ₹1,000 crore milestone (₹1,027.13 crore) - exceeding management's FY26 guidance of ₹9...
items.description_html:
SRGHFL delivered strong Q4 FY26 results with AUM crossing the ₹1,000 crore milestone (₹1,027.13 crore) - exceeding management's FY26 guidance of ₹970 crore. PAT grew 49.4% YoY to ₹9.25 crore with operating leverage evident as cost-to-income improved to 62.84%.

The company achieved its credit rating upgrade trigger and demonstrated superior execution with multiple guidance beats on AUM and cost efficiency.

Positive Factors

AUM crossed ₹1,000 crore milestone to ₹1,027.13 crore, exceeding FY26 guidance of ₹970 crore

PAT grew 49.4% YoY to ₹924.92 Lakh in Q4 FY26

Cost-to-income ratio improved to 62.84% in Q4 from 66.50% in Q4 FY25

PPOP grew 37.5% YoY to ₹1,287.41 Lakh,
demonstrating strong operating leverage

AUM grew 37.5% YoY to ₹1,027.13 crore, meeting credit rating upgrade trigger

NII grew 32.3% to ₹9,826.17 Lakh for full year FY26

Provisions grew only 9.4% YoY despite 37.5% AUM growth, showing stable asset quality

Risk Factors

Finance costs rose 36.7% YoY, slightly faster than interest income growth of 34.8%

Operating cash flow was negative at ₹(24,736.95) Lakh for FY26 due to loan disbursements

Provisions for full year FY26 increased 92.7% YoY to ₹309.72 Lakh

Debt-equity ratio at 2.89x indicates moderate leverage for housing finance company

items.title: IRISDOREME delivered strong revenue growth of 50.4% YoY in Q4 and 30.5% for FY26, exceeding management's expectation of Q4 being the strongest quarter...
items.description_html:
IRISDOREME delivered strong revenue growth of 50.4% YoY in Q4 and 30.5% for FY26, exceeding management's expectation of Q4 being the strongest quarter. However, the company significantly missed its EBITDA margin guidance (15.26% vs 17-18% target) due to a 160% surge in trading purchases and cost pressures.

More critically, the company burned ₹6.70 Cr in operating cash flow despite ₹16.19 Cr PAT, with working capital consuming the entire rights issue proceeds. The FY27 guidance of 40-45% revenue growth appears ambitious given current cash flow constraints.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 50.4% YoY in Q4 to ₹60.48 Cr and
30.5% for FY26 to ₹190.87 Cr

PAT grew 43.5% YoY in Q4 to ₹6.43 Cr and 23.4% for FY26 to ₹16.19 Cr

Net worth increased 72.3% YoY to ₹141.76 Cr following rights issue

Total borrowings reduced 46.5% YoY to ₹20.06 Cr post rights issue

Q4 confirmed as strongest quarter as per management guidance

Risk Factors

FY26 EBITDA margin of 15.26% missed guidance of 17-18% by ~225 bps

Operating cash flow negative at ₹6.70 Cr for FY26 despite ₹16.19 Cr PAT

Trade receivables grew 36.5% YoY, significantly faster than revenue

Purchases of Stock-in-Trade grew 160.2% YoY indicating shift to trading with margin dilution

FY27 revenue guidance of 40-45% growth will require significant working capital and capex

items.title: UPL delivered 17.7% YoY revenue growth to ₹18,335 crore with PBT up 37.4%, but underlying performance was mixed. The Seeds & Post harvest segment con...
items.description_html:
UPL delivered 17.7% YoY revenue growth to ₹18,335 crore with PBT up 37.4%, but underlying performance was mixed. The Seeds & Post harvest segment confirmed a strong turnaround with 40.3% EBIT growth and 397 bps margin expansion, while the dominant Crop Protection segment saw margin compression of 252 bps.

Normalized PAT declined 3.6% YoY, indicating the reported growth was largely a base effect. Strong cash generation (3.54x CFO/PAT) enabled 7% debt reduction, but working capital strain and rising net debt remain concerns. No FY27 guidance was provided.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 17.7% YoY to ₹18,335 crore with strong sequential recovery

PBT surged 37.4% YoY to ₹1,892 crore driven by lower finance costs

Seeds & Post harvest segment revenue grew 20.7% YoY with margins expanding 397 bps to 28.3%

Total debt reduced by 7.0% to ₹22,046 crore with deleveraging efforts

Operating cash flow strong at ₹7,855 crore with 3.54x CFO/PAT ratio

Free cash flow robust at ₹5,449 crore

Risk Factors

Crop Protection segment EBIT grew only 1.3% YoY with 252 bps margin compression despite 17.5% revenue growth

Normalized PAT declined 3.6% YoY indicating reported growth was driven by lower base from FY25 exceptional charges

Working capital investment increased significantly - Inventories up 22.9% and Trade Receivables up 15.3% YoY

Net Debt increased 12.9% to ₹16,071 crore despite gross debt reduction due to 37% cash decline

EBITDA margin contracted 133 bps YoY to 19.0% due to higher operating costs

Impairment loss on financial assets spiked 1795% to ₹379 crore

items.title: SHYAMMETL delivered a strong Q4 with revenue growth of 26.3% YoY (exceeding 15% guidance) and significant margin recovery to 14.4% from 12.2% in Q3. P...
items.description_html:
SHYAMMETL delivered a strong Q4 with revenue growth of 26.3% YoY (exceeding 15% guidance) and significant margin recovery to 14.4% from 12.2% in Q3. PAT grew 41.5% YoY with strong cash conversion (1.91x PAT). New capacity additions (Cold Rolling Mill Phase II, Blast Furnace) are now operational, supporting the achievable FY27 EBITDA target of ₹2,800-3,200 Cr.

Key risks include raw material cost pressure (materials cost up 32.6% YoY) and working capital stress (inventories up 50.8% YoY).

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 22.4% YoY to ₹18,552.21 Cr, exceeding management's 15% CAGR target

Q4 EBITDA margin improved to 14.4% from 12.2% in Q3 (+220 bps QoQ)

PAT grew 41.5% YoY to ₹311.54 Cr in Q4, EPS at ₹11.20

Operating cash flow strong at 1.91x PAT at ₹2,023.56 Cr

New capacities (Cold Rolling Mill Phase II, Blast Furnace) now commissioned

Balance sheet remains robust with low D/E ratio of 0.085x

FY27 EBITDA guidance of ₹2,800-3,200 Cr is achievable with 10-26% growth

Risk Factors

Cost of materials grew 32.6% YoY, outpacing revenue growth of 26.3% YoY

Working capital stress: inventories up 50.8% YoY causing ₹1,516 Cr cash blockage

Other income declined 46.1% YoY to ₹29.22 Cr in Q4

Effective tax rate rose to 31.7% in Q4 from 25.9% prior year

ED provisional attachment of ₹152.48 Cr on subsidiary creates regulatory overhang

Finance costs increased 33.6% YoY to ₹192.23 Cr

items.title: DBCORP delivered mixed Q4 results with strong quarterly performance - revenue grew 5.25% YoY and PAT jumped 18.84% YoY with 183 bps margin expansion. ...
items.description_html:
DBCORP delivered mixed Q4 results with strong quarterly performance - revenue grew 5.25% YoY and PAT jumped 18.84% YoY with 183 bps margin expansion. However, full-year revenue was flat (+0.7%) and PAT declined 10.5%, missing guidance on circulation growth and digital expansion targets.

The core print business showed resilience with 6.1% revenue growth and 354 bps margin expansion, while radio segment remained weak. Balance sheet remains robust with strong net cash position of ₹8,551 million.

Positive Factors

Q4 FY26 revenue grew 5.25% YoY to ₹5,763.91 million

Q4 PAT grew 18.84% YoY to ₹621.92 million

EBITDA margin expanded 183 bps YoY to 20.40%

Operating cash flow was 1.10x PAT at ₹3,652.08 million

Net cash position strengthened to ₹8,551.23 million

Print segment EBIT margin expanded 354 bps YoY to 14.9%

Free cash flow generation of ₹2,065.92 million

Risk Factors

Full-year revenue was flat at 0.7% YoY, missing growth expectations

Full-year PAT declined 10.5% YoY to ₹3,319.99 million

Circulation growth missed guidance at 0.36% vs 3-4%
target

Digital MAUs at ~20 million vs 50 million target - significantly behind

Radio business revenue declined 6.9% YoY with margin compression

Newsprint prices increased contrary to guidance of range-bound

items.title: Urban Company🔻10% post results India Consumer Services excluding InstaHelp grew 26% YoY in Q4 to 808 Cr, which was the fastest growth in 11 quarter...
items.description_html:
Urban Company🔻10% post results

India Consumer Services excluding InstaHelp grew 26% YoY in Q4 to 808 Cr, which was the fastest growth in 11 quarters.

Adjusted EBITDA margin improved to 3.3% of NTV vs 1.6% last year

For FY26, core India business delivered 131 Cr of adjusted EBITDA at 4.1% of NTV

This is important because the business was loss making in FY24

International business is also scaling well
UAE & Singapore delivered 84% NTV growth in Q4 to 211 Cr.

For FY26, international NTV grew 75% to 700 Cr, business turned adjusted EBITDA positive at 6 Cr

Native is also moving in the right direction

NTV grew 67% YoY in Q4 to 89 Cr
For FY26, Native NTV stood at 345 Cr, growing 122% YoY

Adjusted EBITDA loss narrowed from 39 Cr in FY25 to 31 Cr in FY26

The only major drag is InstaHelp

InstaHelp did not exist at the start of FY26, but already delivered 2.7 Mn orders & 40 Cr NTV in Q4

March alone crossed 1.1 Mn orders
However, the business reported 119 Cr adjusted EBITDA loss in Q4

This loss is mainly due to customer acquisition, network density subsidies & supply onboarding

For FY26, the core business ex-InstaHelp generated 106 Cr adj EBITDA

Management has retained its target of cons. adj EBITDA breakeven by Q3FY28 & 1,000 Cr adj EBITDA by FY31 (Valuations are no way cheap)

InstaHelp is the only large investment drag today
At present, InstaHelp is losing Rs 441 per order (that is insane), but it is necessary because the company is aggressively investing to build the category

items.title: RT CSEINDIA: In the remote forest villages of Sugadongar and Semardema in Simdega, Jharkhand, tribal communities lived for decades without electricity...
items.description_html:
RT CSEINDIA
In the remote forest villages of Sugadongar and Semardema in Simdega, Jharkhand, tribal communities lived for decades without electricity, even long after India’s Independence. Hidden deep inside the forests, these villages remained disconnected from reliable power for years.

Only in recent years have these communities finally received electricity through solar mini-grids, bringing light to homes and transforming everyday tribal life.

In this episode of the series, Solar Villages, Down To Earth travels into rural Jharkhand to document stories of resilience, isolation, and how clean energy is changing lives in one of India’s most remote regions.

Coming soon on @down2earthindia's Youtube channel.

items.description_html:
The energy transition isn't coming. It's here. 🌱⚡

India's conventional electricity industry just posted its worst quarter in years:

→ Revenue: -6.18% YoY (Dec 2025)
→ Net profit: -24.3%
→ Thermal generation falling for 4 straight quarters

Meanwhile, renewables jumped +19.5% the 6th consecutive double-digit quarter.

Even India's largest power producer NTPC saw 3 straight quarters of declining sales. Adani Power? Down 16.9% in Dec alone.

Power demand is still growing (+1.8% in Mar '26). Demand is fine. Thermal just isn't getting the call anymore.

Coal's slow goodbye has officially begun. 🔚

items.title: Very Unique disclosure by recently listed Sedemac To help investors make better-informed buy/hold/sell decisions, the company has instituted the follo...
items.description_html:
Very Unique disclosure by recently listed Sedemac

To help investors make better-informed buy/hold/sell decisions, the company has instituted the following framework. A once-a-year survey is conducted, seeking opinions of select institutional investors and analysts — deemed to have a mature understanding of the business — on the degree of risk across key identified risks. This is followed by a management commentary on select risks during the May investor/earnings call.

This year's survey was conducted between May 1–8, 2026. Invitations were extended to CIOs, Fund Managers, and Analysts across 20 buy-side funds and 3 sell-side analysts. Of the 20 buy-side funds, 10 were DIIs (including 5 of the top 6 mutual funds by AUM), 5 were FIIs, and 5 were pre-IPO investors with significant current holdings. To preserve confidentiality, the survey was conducted anonymously by default, with an option for participants to disclose their identity.

Participants assessed 13 business risks, rating each as Low / Medium / High / No View, based on both the probability of the risk materializing and the severity of its potential impact.

They intend to comment on the highlighted risks in our investor/earnings call likely to held in May 2026.

no reco strictly

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Macquarie on SBI O-P, TP Rs 1150 4Q FY26: Earnings miss driven by weak margins and trading losses NIM impacted by higher proportion of...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Macquarie on SBI
O-P, TP Rs 1150
4Q FY26: Earnings miss driven by weak margins and trading losses
NIM impacted by higher proportion of floating book
Asset quality remains a bright spot
Stock trades at 1.6x FY27E P/BV, which appears comfortable given expected ROEs of 15%.

JPM on SBI
OW, TP 1225
SBI reported a weaker than expected 4Q with NII (+3.8% y/y, -2% q/q) at Rs 443.8bn moderating vs +9% y/y in 3Q26 and coming 6% below JPM.
SBI recorded a 17bps q/q decline in NIM (whole bank) to 2.81% and an 18bps q/q decline in NIM (domestic) to 2.93%, vs. Street expectations of flattish to marginally lower margins q/q. However, SBI remains confident of NIM recovery in FY27 vs 4Q backed by multiple steps highlighted below.
Asset quality was benign with credit costs (on avg loans) at 24bps (-16bps q/q), although slippages (+3bps q/q) edged up slightly on seasonality.

MS on SBI
EW, TP Rs 980
Focus on call was on sharp NIM decline.
Management expects some recovery in NIM from 4Q level driven largely by loan yield recovery
Assume some recovery, yet cut F27- 28 NIM forecast by >20bp
Trim F27 & F28 EPS forecasts by 4% & 2%
Management guided to FY27 loan growth of 13-15%, assuming no material macro deterioration

CLSA on SBI
O-P TP Rs 1275
Reported a 4% beat on its 4QFY26 PBT driven by lower-than-expected opex and credit costs & a higher recovery from written-off accounts.
However, on top line, it was a weak quarter, with NII missing estimates by 5%.
This was driven by a 17bp sequential compression in NIM, sharper than expected
Calculated loan yields dropped 40bp to 7.5% driven by repo rate cuts, MCLR repricing & an increase in EBLR share.
Apart from NIM, it was a good quarter.
Loan growth of 17% YoY was best in class, while deposit growth picked up from 9% to 11% YoY
CASA ratio was steady YoY at 39%.
Slippages were steady at 0.5%, & credit costs were 0.2%.

BoFA Sec on SBI
Buy, TP Rs 1200
PAT growth of 6% YoY led by strong loan growth and lower credit cost offset by NIM compression and lower non‑interest income
NIM contraction led by repo‑linked loan repricing, MCLR resets post rate cuts, and mix shift towards T‑bill‑linked loans
FY27 guidance - 13-15% loan growth, full year NIM >3% , <50 bps credit cost and 1%+ RoA

Bernstein on SBI
Market Perform, TP Rs 1300
SBI delivered a mixed 4Q26
Loan growth accelerated to 17% YoY but NIM saw a sharp 18 bps QoQ decline to 2.93%, steepest compression amongst large banks.
Slippages rose sequentially, though largely seasonal, while headline GNPA improved to 1.49%.
RoA moderated 12 bps QoQ to 1.07%, weighed down by NIM compression & a Treasury MTM loss.
Management retained FY27 guidance of 13–15% credit growth, >3% domestic NIM & 50 bps credit costs
Management reaffirmed its through-cycle commitment to 1%+ RoA & 15%+ RoE

UBS on SBI
Neutral, TP Rs 1080
NII declined QoQ as margins dip 18bp QoQ due to loan pricing cut
Loan growth at c5% QoQ led by Corporates; asset quality stable
Management guides for loan growth of 13-15% for FY27; credit cost of c50bp
Expect bank's ROA to see downward pressure as treasury gains and credit costs normalize while margin trends remain subdued
Thus estimate ROA of 0.95% over FY27-28E (vs c1.12% in FY26) & believe risk reward at current valuations (1.2x FY27E P/BV) seems fair
Kotak Inst Eqt on SBI

Buy, TP Rs 1250
SBI reported about 5% yoy earnings growth, led by a 55% yoy decline in provisions, while operating profits declined about 10% yoy
Bank reported solid loan growth (~17% yoy), low credit costs (~25 bps) and a NIM decline of 20 bps qoq
Miss against expectations is likely to prompt a near-term pause in its re-rating, with investors awaiting evidence of recovery before rerating bank.
@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh

items.title: Overseas investors are piling into US stocks: Foreign investors now allocate a record 63% of their US financial assets to equities. This percentage ha...
items.description_html:
Overseas investors are piling into US stocks:

Foreign investors now allocate a record 63% of their US financial assets to equities.

This percentage has more than DOUBLED since the 2008 Financial Crisis.

This also surpasses the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble peak by ~10 percentage points.

By comparison, the long-term average allocation is ~40%.

As a result, foreign investors now own a record $21.3 trillion in US stocks and equity funds, surging +170% since 2020.

Foreign investors are doubling down on US markets.

items.title: China is quietly reshaping the global oil market: China's crude oil imports fell -20% MoM in April, to 8.2 million barrels per day, the lowest in at l...
items.description_html:
China is quietly reshaping the global oil market:

China's crude oil imports fell -20% MoM in April, to 8.2 million barrels per day, the lowest in at least 2 years.

This is down -30%, or -3.5 million barrels per day, from pre-war levels of ~11.7 million barrels per day.

To put this into perspective, the decline nearly matches the daily total oil consumption of Japan.

This is also 2 times larger than the volume supplied by the UAE pipeline that bypasses the Strait of Hormuz.

Furthermore, Chinese state-owned oil companies have been reselling crude cargoes to European and Asian buyers, a sign that inventory levels are comfortable despite a global supply shortage.

China is supporting the global oil market even amid severe shortages.

items.title: RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش: I hope these messages sink in within the administration: A. Iran does not believe it l...
items.description_html:
RT Danny (Dennis) Citrinowicz ,داني سيترينوفيتش
I hope these messages sink in within the administration:

A. Iran does not believe it lost this confrontation. On the contrary, from Tehran’s perspective, it proved resilience and strategic staying power.

B. Iran has no intention of capitulating or accepting Washington’s demands, not now, and not in the foreseeable future.

C. No matter how much Trump threatens “the end of civilization,” Iran is unlikely to back down. Even if military confrontation resumes, Tehran is not expected to reverse course under pressure alone.

D. The only realistic paths to an agreement are either:
compromising on key Iranian demands, or pursuing regime change in Iran.

If the administration is unwilling to commit the enormous military, political, and economic resources required for regime change, then it likely lacks the leverage to force Tehran to accept maximalist terms.

E. Neither sanctions, blockades, nor other “silver bullet” pressure tactics are likely to compel Iran to fundamentally alter its negotiating position. Claims otherwise are increasingly detached from reality.

F. Iran and its regional proxies retain significant capacity to inflict economic and strategic pain on Gulf states, particularly in the energy and maritime domains.

G. Any agreement with Iran is unlikely to include meaningful restrictions on its missile program or regional proxy network, and will almost certainly acknowledge, at least implicitly:
Iran’s right to enrich uranium, and its Hormuz straits control.

H. Most Gulf states are deeply concerned about escalation and understand that toppling the Iranian regime would be extraordinarily difficult and destabilizing.

I. The United States did not achieve a decisive strategic victory. Despite operational successes by both the U.S. and Israel, the broader strategic balance in the region has not fundamentally changed.

J. Iran is not Venezuela. It is a far larger, more institutionalized, ideologically committed, and strategically resilient state with deep regional networks and a much higher tolerance for prolonged confrontation.

The bottom line us simple: There is an illusion in Washington that Iran emerged weakened, isolated, and ultimately cornered by military pressure, sanctions, and the threat of escalation. From Tehran’s perspective, the recent confrontation did not end in defeat. Quite the opposite. The Islamic Republic believes it demonstrated resilience, survivability, and an ability to absorb enormous pressure without surrendering politically. In the eyes of Iran’s leadership, simply enduring against the combined pressure of the United States and Israel reinforces the regime’s central narrative: that resistance works.

The administration must recognize an uncomfortable reality: coercion has limits. If the United States truly seeks to compel Iran to abandon its core strategic doctrine, there are only two possible paths. The first is compromise, meaning accepting that any sustainable agreement will have to accommodate at least some Iranian red lines. The second is regime chang. No more no http://less.No middle ground.

#Iran
#IranWar‌


Ali Hashem علي هاشم: Unacceptable… so?

BREAKING: Iran responds to the US with a "10-point" message on the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz:

The full 10-point response:

1. US military presence is described as the "main source" of instability

2. American bases are portrayed as "unable to secure themselves"

3. Iran says the Strait of Hormuz should be free of the US

4. Tehran describes a "shared destiny" with Gulf countries

5. Foreign powers are declared unwelcome in the Persian Gulf

6. Iran says its growing influence is part of a new regional order

7. Controlling of the Strait of Hormuz is explained as key to security

8. Iran says it wants to end "hostile exploitation" of Hormuz

9. New rules and management are promised to boost regional prosperity

10. Tehran says the new strategy would benefit the Iranian economy

President Trump on the response just now: "I don't like it."

If India is better why is foreign capital not flooding in? Why is INR in crisis?

items.title: BREAKING: Money market funds posted +$136 billion in inflows last week, the largest weekly intake since January 2026. This is also the 2nd-largest wee...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Money market funds posted +$136 billion in inflows last week, the largest weekly intake since January 2026.

This is also the 2nd-largest weekly inflow since the beginning of 2025.

This follows -$175 billion in outflows in the preceding week, the largest weekly withdrawal on record.

As a result, the 4-week average of outflows stands at -$45 billion, the 2nd-largest on record.

Meanwhile, investors allocated +$25.9 billion to bonds last week, with Investment-Grade bonds alone attracting +$16.4 billion, the largest weekly intake since January 2026.

Investors are shifting capital after a historic run.

items.title: This is a historic divergence: The ratio of the S&P 500 Information Technology sector to the S&P 500 index is up to a record 0.87. This ratio has rise...
items.description_html:
This is a historic divergence:

The ratio of the S&P 500 Information Technology sector to the S&P 500 index is up to a record 0.87.

This ratio has risen +50% since the 2022 bear market low.

Meanwhile, the number of employees in the information technology sector relative to total US nonfarm payrolls is down to 0.02, an all-time low.

Since 2022, this figure has declined -15% as the number of tech payrolls has dropped.

Over this period, the number of employees in tech has fallen -342,000, to 2.77 million, the lowest since February 2021.

Information sector payrolls have now declined for 16 consecutive months, the longest streak since the 2008 Financial Crisis.

The gap between the market and the economy has rarely been this wide.

items.title: Lots of countries announced measures early on to deal with the shortages/high prices due to #IranWar. India had earlier invoked the Essential Commodit...
items.description_html:
Lots of countries announced measures early on to deal with the shortages/high prices due to #IranWar.

India had earlier invoked the Essential Commodities Act to protect & control low LPG supply.

A (non-exhaustive) list of what others have done,

1. Philippines: Declared a state of national emergency due to the imminent danger of critically low energy supply.

2. Bangladesh: Daily fuel purchase limits by vehicle type. For eg - bikes up to 2 litres, pvt cars 10 litres etc. Shorter govt office hours. Early closure of markets & malls. And more.

3. S. Korea: Tightened public-sector vehicle rotation from five days to two days; vehicles restricted every alternate business day due to low fuel supply.

4. Indonesia: Announced fuel rationing; mandated civil servants to work from home once a week amid fuel supply disruptions and petrol-station shortages.

5. Australia: Sought agreements with Asian trade partners to secure refined fuel in return for LNG, as hundreds of service stations ran out of fuel.

6. Slovenia: Set a daily cap on road-fuel purchases at gas stations due to shortages at the pump.

#Nifty #GIFTNIFTY #BankNifty #India #IranWar‌ #Oil #Gold

items.title: I just finished David McCullough's 600-page book on the building of the Panama Canal. There was much less economic history here than I would have pref...
items.description_html:
I just finished David McCullough's 600-page book on the building of the Panama Canal. There was much less economic history here than I would have preferred, but it is nonetheless a great story, even if towards the end there is a little too much trivia. Like most people I am familiar with many of the great French political scandals in the decades before WW1 (Boulanger, Dreyfus, Caillaux, Union Générale), but while I knew that there was a "Panama scandal", I didn't realize its sheer extent until I read this book.

The first half of the book (on de Lesseps and France's attempt to build the canal) is more interesting in many ways than the second half (the US building of the canal), although the political intrigues in the latter are fascinating.

items.title: Piramal Finance - Latest Update & What Has Changed in 1 Year The biggest change in the last 1 year is that the AUM mix transition is now almost comple...
items.description_html:
Piramal Finance - Latest Update & What Has Changed in 1 Year

The biggest change in the last 1 year is that the AUM mix transition is now almost complete

Earlier, the thesis was mainly about legacy book run down & growth book scale-up

Now, that transition is largely done
Total AUM has crossed 1,01,230 Cr, growing 25% YoY

Growth AUM is now 97% of total AUM vs 91% in FY25
Legacy AUM is now only 3% of total AUM vs 9% in FY25

What changed in the last 1 year

- Total AUM moved from 0.8 L Cr in Mar'25 to 1.01 L Cr in Mar'26
- Growth AUM grew 33% YoY
- Retail share increased from 80% to 85%
- Cons. PAT moved from 485 Cr in FY25 to 1,506 Cr in FY26
- FY26 PAT guidance was 1,300-1,500 Cr, actual came at 1,506 Cr

FY27 outlook:
- Total AUM growth - 25%
- Cons. PAT growth - 50%
- Exit quarter ROA - 2.5%

Long term targets:
- AUM target - 1.5 L Cr+ by Mar-28
- ROA target - above 3%

Retail Opex/AUM has fallen from 6.5% in Q4FY23 to 3.6% in Q4FY26

Asset quality remains stable
Retail 90+ DPD stood at 0.6% in Q4FY26

Assessed carry forward losses now stand at around 24,600 Cr

This is a major support for profitability because it can keep PAT closer to PBT for a long period

Once it used to trade at 0.8 P/B now multiples have expanded significantly due to the progress




Equity Insights Elite: Piramal Enterprises

Legacy book run down to <3 % of AUM by FY26
New Growth book already 91 % of AUM

FY26 Guidance
- AUM seen at \~ 1 L Cr (+25 % ), Retail +30 % YoY
- PAT guided at 1300-1500 Cr (vs 0.49 K Cr FY25)
- Growth book ROA : 1.8 % Q4 FY25
- Medium-term target : 2.5-3 %

items.title: Something that I really think about when it comes to tailwinds 1)Large theme+ start early+mediocre investment = Phenomenal gains 2)Exceptional investm...
items.description_html:
Something that I really think about when it comes to tailwinds

1)Large theme+ start early+mediocre investment = Phenomenal gains

2)Exceptional investment+ tailwind missing = Frustration

to quote Madhu Kela " I have come out very frustrated in investing in best companies where the tailwind was missing"

Something to ponder about , it is more variable change of attention that we should give to rate of change +tailwind and not only best player , this I speak in terms of an equity shareholder not a promoter , promoter will continue to benefit by running a great company with headwinds , but an investor wont , as incremental % of profit will be difficult to capture in contrast to a company that is in tailwinds but mediocre ( with base decent management, may not be best , but good ) but rate of change is visible

items.title: I always look for qualitative signals when I research and look deep into any company, a note on asking the right questions : Things like 1) How freque...
items.description_html:
I always look for qualitative signals when I research and look deep into any company, a note on asking the right questions :

Things like
1) How frequently the company talks to the media
2) How often to KMP trade in and out of the stock
3) Does the company spend on digital presence and PR
4) Does hiring growth match guidance for company growth ( Linkedin/Naukri )
5)Suppliers and vendors reputation of the company, credit policy , payment terms , sales rep aggression
6) Does the company keep doing webinars with PMS managers to justify the business
7) Are they ecosystem focused or product focused , to understand the bigger picture with data like and acquisition for further growth close to greenfield plots 8) Nature of Clients with tenure mapping and sectoral profit depth

These are some of the things that we must look into other than the typical B/S and Cash flow analysis also , the business is a moving picture with many parts , to understand the whole picture we must broaden the vision

@ishmohit1

items.title: RT Ananth Rupanagudi: Maharana Thakore Sahib Sir Jaswantsinhji Fatehsinhji (1859-1907), the ruler of Limbdi, a princely state in Kathiawad, was well-k...
items.description_html:
RT Ananth Rupanagudi
Maharana Thakore Sahib Sir Jaswantsinhji Fatehsinhji (1859-1907), the ruler of Limbdi, a princely state in Kathiawad, was well-known for his abilities, sagacity, shrewdness, able administration of state affairs, and for his great consideration for the welfare. His opinion was often sought out by Governors of Bombay, including Sir James Fergusson and Sir Donald Mackay.

A progressive and highly educated ruler (K.C.I.E.), he was notable for modernizing his state, supporting Sanatana Dharma, aiding Swami Vivekananda's trip to the West, and serving on the Bombay Legislative Council.

He was an articulate speaker of English, travelled to England and America, and represented Western Indian princes at Queen Victoria's Jubilee in 1887, where he was appointed a Knight Commander of the Order of the Indian Empire (K.C.I.E.).

In 1891-92, he hosted Swami Vivekananda at the Limbdi palace. It is reported that the Thakore Saheb gave Vivekananda the initial idea to travel to the West to preach Vedanta. He was considered a staunch supporter of Hindu traditions (Sanatana Dharma) while holding progressive views on education and social issues. #Gujarat #royals #SwamiVivekananda #SanatanaDharma

items.title: Defensive stocks have never been this disliked: The healthcare sector now accounts for just 8.3% of the S&P 500’s market cap, the lowest percentage s...
items.description_html:
Defensive stocks have never been this disliked:

The healthcare sector now accounts for just 8.3% of the S&P 500’s market cap, the lowest percentage since 1994.

Their weight has fallen by -50% since the 2022 bear market.

By comparison, healthcare represented ~9.0% of the index’s value at the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble peak.

Furthermore, consumer staples, healthcare, and utilities collectively now account for just ~15% of the S&P 500’s market cap, the lowest since at least the 1970s.

Their weighting has dropped -12 percentage points since 2022, marking an even bigger drop than during the Dot-Com run.

Tech stocks have never been bigger.

items.description_html:
BREAKING: Global physical gold-backed ETFs posted +$6.6 billion in inflows in April.

European funds led, at +$3.7 billion, followed by Asia, at +$1.8 billion, and North America, at +$1.0 billion.

This marks a sharp recovery from -$12.0 billion in outflows in March, the largest monthly withdrawal on record.

Year-to-date, global gold ETFs have attracted +$19.0 billion in inflows.

This lifted total assets under management across global gold ETFs by +1% MoM, to $615 billion.

At the same time, gold holdings jumped +45 tonnes, to 4,137 tonnes, the 3rd-highest on record.

Global gold demand is rapidly recovering.

items.title: RT Anthony DiGiorgio, DO, MHA: This is the ultimate midwit healthcare take. No, 32 countries have not “figured out” universal healthcare. The UK has...
items.description_html:
RT Anthony DiGiorgio, DO, MHA
This is the ultimate midwit healthcare take.

No, 32 countries have not “figured out” universal healthcare.

The UK has “free” healthcare, and roughly 1 in 3 cancer patients in England still fail to start treatment within 62 days of urgent referral.

Canada has “free” healthcare, and the median wait for neurosurgical treatment is around a year.

Australia has “free” healthcare, and over half the country still buys private insurance despite paying for a public universal system with their taxes.

Switzerland has universal coverage, because residents are required to buy private insurance. There is no government system where benevolent bureaucrats tuck you in at night with a warm blanket and an MRI appointment.

The actual lesson from other wealthy countries is not “they figured it out.”

America’s system has huge problems. Our prices are insane, insurance markets are distorted, and hospital systems are cartelized. Our regulations make care more expensive than it needs to be.

Yet we still guarantee access to even the 8% who don’t have coverage. We give easy routes to qualify for medicaid for those with disabilities.

Pretending the rest of the world solved healthcare because they slapped the word “universal” on a rationing scheme is not analysis.

It is bumper sticker policy for people who think access means having a card in your wallet while you wait a year to see the doctor you need.


daz: Free Universal Healthcare is so complicated and expensive that only 32 of the 33 wealthiest countries in the world have figured it out.

items.title: RT The Caravan: Jaswinder Singh, a 35-year-old BSF soldier was examined for the last time around 12.15 pm on 18 March, according to that day’s medica...
items.description_html:
RT The Caravan
Jaswinder Singh, a 35-year-old BSF soldier was examined for the last time around 12.15 pm on 18 March, according to that day’s medical report. “Accused is physically fit after clinical examination in my opinion,” the doctor wrote, after checking his blood pressure, pulse and oxygen levels. He further noted that Jaswinder had been “conscious, cooperative well oriented.” A few hours later, Madan Lal, the third additional sessions judge in Jammu, recorded that Jaswinder had been physically produced at 2.10 pm and remanded him to the custody of the NCB until 23 March.

A little over thirty-six hours after Jaswinder’s last medical examination—around 2.45 am on 20 March—Lovejeet Kaur, his wife received a phone call from Aakash Rai, who told her that Jaswinder’s blood pressure had dropped significantly and that he had been taken to Pulse Hospital in Amritsar, located over two hundred kilometres away. He asked the family to come to Amritsar. “I thought he was lying so that they can harass us more. Perhaps it was a trap to call us,” Lovejeet said. She pressed further—there were many hospitals in Jammu, she said, so why take him to Amritsar just because his blood pressure fell? “He kept saying that I should come and then cut the call.”

When the family finally saw Jaswinder in the mortuary of the civil hospital in Amritsar, they found his body riddled with bruises and welts. But only when they removed his clothes and turned him on his stomach did they witness the true horror.

Read Jatinder Kaur Tur's (@jatinder_tur) report in The Caravan's May 2026 issue on how a soldier of the Border Security Force died in NCB custody: https://caravanmagazine.in/crime/a-soldier-of-the-border-security-force-dies-in-ncb-custody #BSF #NCB

RT Gregory Brew
I hear this quite a lot--if Iran can withstand US pressure, what's to stop them from continuing this standoff indefinitely?

The leadership wants a deal to end the war--resolving the confrontation on favorable terms is the endgame, and ultimately why they fought the war as aggressively as they did.

A perpetual state of "no war, no peace" is not their preference. Nor is it sustainable.


Gregg Carlstrom: At the end of the day, though, Iran desperately needs a way out of its economic doom loop. If it can't translate tactical wins into a deal that provides significant sanctions relief, stubbornness may end up looking like a self-defeating strategy

items.title: Urban Company has stepped up incentives for both consumers and home service partners in its InstaHelp business during the January–March quarter - the...
items.description_html:
Urban Company has stepped up incentives for both consumers and home service partners in its InstaHelp business during the January–March quarter - the biggest contributor to its losses.

Loss per order increased by 17% to Rs 447, even as net order value declined.

Total losses from InstaHelp nearly doubled to Rs 119 crore, while orders grew 66% to 2.7 million in the January–March quarter compared to October–December.

It will be interesting to see how retention metrics evolve in this category. Some stats for InstaHelp:

- Monthly user retention is 30–33%, with users placing around 2.5 orders per month

- The top 10% of monthly users placed around 8 orders, while the top 1% placed around 21 orders in March 2026

items.title: "The Renaissance Required in West Bengal" - my article in today's New Indian Express where I argue that Bengal's economic revival depends on recapturi...
items.description_html:
"The Renaissance Required in West Bengal" - my article in today's New Indian Express where I argue that Bengal's economic revival depends on recapturing its civilizational identity after decades of deliberate manipulation. As the civilizational roots of Bengalis were deliberately chipped away, it left an intellectual and socio-cultural vacuum. The sneering of “aanteil” intellectuals cannot fill the emptiness. This is the source of Bengal’s political anarchism, mindless trade unionism, militant student activism and deadwood intellectualism; all merely trying to fill that hole in the heart. As an aside, a similar story can be told of another state - Punjab, the other state that was Partitioned.

https://www.newindianexpress.com/opinion/2026/May/08/the-renaissance-required-in-west-bengal-again

items.title: Don’t chase commodity chemicals after they’ve already run up significantly from recent lows. Think in terms of probabilities, not binaries. Avoid tr...
items.description_html:
Don’t chase commodity chemicals after they’ve already run up significantly from recent lows. Think in terms of probabilities, not binaries. Avoid trying to predict the next big move. If you own them from lower levels, feel free to hold, provided your thesis was never about just 2 months.

Several detailed presentations covered commodity chemicals ranging from NOCIL to DMCC and Deepak Nitrite. There are 30 other commodity chemical stocks, but the same framework applies to all.

Heuristics work when rules fail. Point #11 is what I seriously follow. Sold Indoco Remedies yesterday at day's high as it failed this test:

1. Think in probabilities instead of making absolute predictions about commodity stocks.
2. Do build positions gradually rather than chasing a stock after a sharp 40-50% rally.
3. Do continuously update your view as new supply, demand, and geopolitical information emerges.
4. Do focus on whether the market has already priced in the bullish narrative before buying. Predictions usually fail, so have a margin of safety!
5. Do preserve capital and wait patiently if the risk-reward becomes unfavourable after a fast/euphoric move.
6. Don’t chase vertically rising stocks out of fear of missing out.
7. Don’t extrapolate recent gains indefinitely into the future, because commodity cycles often reverse sharply.
8. Don’t confuse a compelling geopolitical story with a guaranteed investment outcome.
9. Don’t ignore demand destruction risk when commodity prices rise too quickly.
10. Don’t become emotionally attached to a bullish thesis when market conditions start changing.
11. Focus on the balance sheet, cash flows and people (management) behind those stocks!


Antifragile Thinking: Commodity chemicals and pharma APIs could surprise on the upside. Cyclical, short-term trade. Not a buy and sleep. Buy to sell. Buy fast. Sell faster. Stick to what you understand. Ignore social validation.

items.title: RT Karim Fawaz: Chinese oil imports down 2.5 mb/d, US oil (crude + product exports) up 2+ mb/d, that's a 4.5+ mb/d swing in global oil balances coming...
items.description_html:
RT Karim Fawaz
Chinese oil imports down 2.5 mb/d, US oil (crude + product exports) up 2+ mb/d, that's a 4.5+ mb/d swing in global oil balances coming from the two largest demand markets. It may not be sustainable or sufficient to solve the physical crisis, but it's a massive relief valve.


Javier Blas: COLUMN: China is quietly slashing oil imports, an invisible hand that's rebalancing the market in the middle of the Hormuz closure.

(The shift has not only capped benchmark oil prices, but also triggered a collapse in physical differentials)

@Opinion

https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2026-05-08/iran-war-china-s-invisible-hand-is-rebalancing-the-oil-market?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter

items.title: How the GRID ETF has done over the last year and is a proxy to Electrification What is the GRID ETF? GRID (First Trust NASDAQ Clean Edge Smart Grid In...
items.description_html:
How the GRID ETF has done over the last year and is a proxy to Electrification

What is the GRID ETF?

GRID (First Trust NASDAQ Clean Edge Smart Grid Infrastructure Index Fund) works as a clean proxy for the electrification theme because its portfolio is built around the companies that physically make the grid run I.e. transformer makers, switchgear suppliers, transmission equipment manufacturers, smart meter firms, and industrial automation players.

Top holdings include names like ABB, Schneider Electric, Eaton, Quanta Services, Johnson Controls, Aptiv, and Prysmian. Essentially the global bench of electrical equipment and grid engineering leaders that utilities and industrials have to buy from when they upgrade transmission and distribution networks.

Trends it benefits from:-

With power demand rising from reshored manufacturing, EV charging build-outs, and higher household electrification, aging grids need massive replacement and expansion capex, and GRID captures that spend directly at the equipment layer rather than at the generation end. That is what makes it more of a "picks and shovels" vehicle for electrification than a bet on any single utility or renewable developer.

items.title: RT Pritesh Gupta: most debates fail before they even begin because the problem is framed wrong from the start. people think they are arguing about sol...
items.description_html:
RT Pritesh Gupta
most debates fail before they even begin because the problem is framed wrong from the start.

people think they are arguing about solutions, but often they are only reacting to symptoms. the real issue stays hidden underneath. a good framework helps you move from what is visible to what is actually causing it.

this is why many indian systems remain stuck. the focus stays on inputs: money spent, schemes launched, announcements made. but inputs are not outcomes. distributing tablets is not the same as children learning. activity gets measured because it is easy to count. outcomes get ignored because they are harder to see.

whoever defines the problem usually controls the solution. frame a learning crisis as an infrastructure issue and the answer becomes buildings and devices. frame it as an information issue and the answer becomes better data, feedback, and accountability. same reality, different response.

that is why framing is not just intellectual, it is political. the way a problem is named decides who gets blamed, what progress looks like, and whose reality matters. which is why getting the question right is often harder, and far more important, than finding an answer.

check out: http://frameworks.pranaykotas.com by @pranaykotas

items.title: SBI 4QFY26 Results: First Cut: Margin slips sharply dragging down profitability *SBI reported 9% PAT miss at ~Rs197bn mainly due to lower NII growth 4...
items.description_html:
SBI 4QFY26 Results: First Cut: Margin slips sharply dragging down profitability

*SBI reported 9% PAT miss at ~Rs197bn mainly due to lower NII growth 4%YoY/-6% QoQ, other income and higher other opex.

* Credit growth was strong and ahead of expectations at 17.2% YoY / 5.4% QoQ, the highest in the last eleven quarters. However, this was mainly driven by corporates and thus hurt loan yields.

* Deposit growth improved to 11% YoY / ~5% QoQ which is ahead of expectations too with stable LDR QoQ at 82%.

* NIM declined by 18bps QoQ to 2.81%, likely due to lower loan yield and higher cost of funds. There is some reclassification of interest income but does not much affects margins.

* Slippages were slightly higher at 0.47% but headline asset quality continued to improve, with 16bps QoQ decline in GNPA ratio to 1.5% (all-time low). NNPA stable QoQ to 0.4% however specific PCR drops by 114 bps to 74%.

* Clarity on margin contraction in 4Q, outlook on growth/margins and forex trade unwinding post RBIs ditkat will be awaited from results

* SBI currently trades at 1.2x FY-28E ABV (ex-subs). Currently, we have a BUY rating on the stock given its strong growth, more than 1% RoA and lower possibility of SBI being part of ensuing PSB consolidation.

items.title: RT Prabhakar Kudva: If you're confused about how to navigate the market, what stocks to buy, what to sell, it is because you dont have a system or an ...
items.description_html:
RT Prabhakar Kudva
If you're confused about how to navigate the market, what stocks to buy, what to sell, it is because you dont have a system or an anchor which can guide your decisions.

Newsflow driven investing can drive us crazy and is extremely unprofitable.

A system gives you coherence in thoughts and the clarity to execute. It need not be rigid and its not meant to be machine like. It should more like an anchor around which you can think clearly and decide.

For me the anchors are earnings and growth that drive my actions.

Start systemizing your investing. It's one of the biggest edges out there.

items.title: RT nikhilv: There are a few reasons for this - Inequality literature in Claude's pretraining data is 'actionable', the model sees this as a problem to...
items.description_html:
RT nikhilv
There are a few reasons for this -
Inequality literature in Claude's pretraining data is 'actionable', the model sees this as a problem to be solved. Conversely, literature around growth is framed descriptively, like matter-of-fact. So the model has learnt that anyone serious about public policy will be more concerned about inequality than growth.
Anthropic's constitution is also to blame - it introduces priors, some explicitly mentioned (avoid harms to vulnerable groups/be fair etc - inequality concerns follow naturally). And then you have RLHF from US/UK left of centre cohorts, and black box reveals itself.
Try grok and you'll see a mirror image - xAI tuned the priors the other way, so it may flip rather than disappear @pranaykotas @srajagopalan @amitvarma @AshishPonders @acorn


Pranay Kotasthane: A specific example on why cognitive autonomy is even more important in the AI age: I was using Claude Code to annotate public policy frameworks from our newsletter, & I noticed that Claude is pilled on inequality concerns. Talks less about growth but always inserts inequality as

items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
The Cracks Are Widening at Tata Group

At a time of financial challenges across key Tata Group companies the Tata top guard is intent on unseating each other.

The conspiracy and chaos at Tata Sons isn’t going to win it any friends at the regulator.

Worse, it may invite political intervention.

Unlike other charitable trusts in India, Tata Trusts have a rare privilege. It’s not inconceivable for that to be withdrawn.

Despite all these vulnerabilities, the cracks that opened up in the Tata Group 10 years ago are only widening.

items.title: RT India Wildlife News: Jabalpur has confirmed it. Canine distemper virus killed tigress T-141 and her four cubs at Kanha in April — five tigers dead...
items.description_html:
RT India Wildlife News
Jabalpur has confirmed it. Canine distemper virus killed tigress T-141 and her four cubs at Kanha in April — five tigers dead in nine days.
The NTCA has had a stray dog vaccination mandate for tiger reserves since 2020. It mandates vaccination within a 5 km radius. It specifies feral dogs caught inside reserves must never be released back.
The policy exists. The five tigers are still dead.
Other MP reserves are now vaccinating in "mission mode" — Panna, Kuno, Satpura. Reactive. The same thing happened after Gir's lion CDV outbreak in 2018. A review followed. An SOP was produced. Six years later, Kanha.
Questions now turn to whether the protocol was being implemented, and whether India's tiger reserves have the veterinary infrastructure to make it work.
IWN report: https://indiawildlifenews.com/2026/05/08/kanha-cdv-canine-distemper-tiger-deaths-ntca-vaccination-policy/
#IndiaWildlifeNews #WhenWildThingsMakeHeadlines #CDV #CanineDistemperVirus #KanhaNationalPark #TigerDeaths #NTCA #MadhyaPradesh #BengalTiger #WildlifeIndia #ConservationPolicy #TigerConservation #WildlifeDisease #StrayDogs #WII

items.title: The rate of murder, or ‘intentional homicide’, is widely used as an indicator of public safety in a city or country. India recorded nearly 37,500 in...
items.description_html:
The rate of murder, or ‘intentional homicide’, is widely used as an indicator of public safety in a city or country.

India recorded nearly 37,500 incidents of intentional homicides in 2023.

The United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) considers statistics put out by India's National Crime Records Bureau (NCRB) under three offences to calculate intentional homicides in India.

First, culpable homicide, that is murder (punishable under Sec. 103 under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita or previously, Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code). Four in five intentional homicides in India are murders.

Second, dowry deaths (defined and punishable under Section 80 of BNS or previously, Section 304B of IPC), accounting for nearly one in six intentional homicides.

The remaining are culpable homicides not amounting to murder (punishable under Sec. 105 under BNS or previously, Section 304 of IPC), an offence that is considered less serious than murder. One example of this would be a sudden fight between two people where one of them kills the other only in the heat of the moment, without any premeditation to cause the death.

How has the incidence of intentional homicide changed over time in India? Read Ameya Bokil’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/homicide/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Murder #Homicide #Law #India #DataForIndia

items.title: Emami’s acquisition of a 60% stake in IncNut values the 15-year-old company at Rs 535 crore. That is below the Rs 850 crore valuation it received in ...
items.description_html:
Emami’s acquisition of a 60% stake in IncNut values the 15-year-old company at Rs 535 crore.

That is below the Rs 850 crore valuation it received in 2022, when it raised funding from VentureEast, according to Tracxn. Other backers include RPSG Ventures.

Its revenues have declined from Rs 232 crore in FY23 to Rs 175 crore in FY25.

It will be interesting to see how IncNut ramps up its valuation for the remaining 40% stake in future tranches while working with Emami.


The Arc: 🚨 Personal and healthcare major Emami is acquiring 60% stake in the owner of D2C brands Vedix and Skinkraft for Rs 321 crore.

The remaining stake in IncNut, the parent company, will be acquired over four years based on performance.

items.description_html:
Interesting to see early Google backer Ram Shriram’s venture fund, Sherpalo, lead a major funding round in an Indian startup after over a decade.

Sherpalo was very active in India during the 2006–2010 period, backing companies like Info Edge, Cleartrip, and MapmyIndia.

It sold its India portfolio in 2014 to Lightbox, which was run by its former India head.

Since then, Sherpalo has participated in a few funding rounds but has not taken a lead role.

Sherpalo first backed Skyroot in 2022 and is now doubling down.


The Arc: 🚨 Skyroot becomes India's first space-tech Unicorn as it raises $60 mn at $1.1 bn valuation.

Round led by early Google-backer Sherpalo, along with Singapore's GIC.

The eight-year-old startup was founded by former ISRO scientists Pawan Kumar Chandana and Naga Bharath Daka.

items.title: RT Anish Moonka: Look down at your big toe. If it used to have hair on it and now it doesn't, blood is probably struggling to reach down that far. Doc...
items.description_html:
RT Anish Moonka
Look down at your big toe. If it used to have hair on it and now it doesn't, blood is probably struggling to reach down that far. Doctors have used this as an early warning sign for decades, and the test that confirms it takes five minutes.

Hair grows because tiny blood vessels feed each follicle with oxygen and nutrients. When fatty deposits clog the arteries higher up your leg, blood barely makes it to your toes. The follicles starve and shut down. Hair falls out and stops coming back.

This is called peripheral artery disease, or PAD, and roughly 200 million people worldwide have it. Mayo Clinic, Johns Hopkins, and the UK's NHS all list bare feet and lower legs as a recognized warning sign. More than half of them feel completely normal in their legs. The hair on their toes is often the only thing giving the disease away.

Hair loss alone doesn't mean you have PAD. Sock friction, age, and hormones can thin foot hair on their own. Doctors look at the bigger picture: shiny tight skin on the calves, cold feet that won't warm up, cuts that take forever to heal, a weak pulse behind your ankle bone, slow-growing toenails. If a few of those signs are happening at once, the hair loss starts to mean something.

The same fatty buildup clogging your leg arteries usually clogs the ones feeding your heart and brain. People with PAD are roughly twice as likely to die of a heart attack or stroke over the next ten years as people without it. Even when they have zero leg symptoms. One long study followed silent PAD patients for a decade and found that only 3 out of every 5 were still alive at the end.

The test is called the Ankle-Brachial Index. A nurse takes your blood pressure at your ankle, then at your arm, and divides one by the other. If the ankle reading is much lower, blood is struggling to reach your feet. The cutoff doctors use is 0.9. Five minutes, doesn't hurt, costs almost nothing.

What turns this from "probably nothing" into "go get checked" is the usual cardiovascular crowd. Smoking, diabetes, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, over 50. If a few of those describe you and your toes have gone smooth, ask your doctor for the ABI on your next visit. If they look puzzled, say "ankle blood pressure compared to arm blood pressure." They'll know.

Your toes give away clogged arteries before anything else for one reason. They're at the end of the line.

items.description_html: How incumbents are solving the imference-memory problem is so interesting. Model designs & architectures are evolving so fast & likes of NVDA are already investing heavily. The memory issue is mainly because of the transformers & attention problem. I read a very interesting study on state space models too. As model architecture slowly starts to mimic human brain, the inference costs will collapse to zero and no memory issues either. As an investor, you should follow this space closely to know when you call it out.

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: Every earnings season, thousands of investors run the same stock screeners: PE < 20 ✅ ROE > 15 ✅ Growth > 20% ✅ Then they...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
Every earnings season, thousands of investors run the same stock screeners:
PE < 20 ✅
ROE > 15 ✅
Growth > 20% ✅
Then they think they’ve found a great stock idea.
What they’ve actually found is a list.

In 1972, Warren Buffett bought See’s Candies — a business no screener would have highlighted.
The numbers looked ordinary, but what Buffett saw was pricing power, customer behaviour, and emotional moat.

That’s the difference between filtering for numbers and filtering for businesses.

This Sunday, we’ll show you how to build a framework to identify truly exceptional businesses — Indian & Global.

✶ Topic: How to Filter Epic Stock Ideas? Indian & International Businesses
✶ Date & Time: 10 May, 1 p.m. (IST)
✶ Registration Link: https://learn.soic.in/learn/fast-checkout/269081?priceId=266657
Use a browser to enrol, mobile apps charge 30% additional fees
✶ Venue: Zoom
☞ Enrolment valid for 365 days.
☞ Recording available 48-72 hours after the live webinar.

What we will cover:
➥ Why are most idea generation processes broken — and what to replace them with?
➥ The industry-first lens: how to identify sectors where the next leg of growth is already visible?
➥ What global businesses teach Indian investors.
➥ SOIC’s framework for spotting high-conviction ideas.
➥ Live breakdowns of Indian & international companies.

This isn’t a tips session.
It’s about building the muscle to generate your own ideas — consistently and intelligently.

📌 NOTE:
Our SOIC Membership & StockScans bundle includes all our webinars & micro-courses.
Explore to join: https://learn.soic.in/learn/SOIC-Course
Coupon Code: SOICSCANS

items.title: I think this is genuinely strange as a question to ask. Gamestop is buying EBay for $55bn - but it's a merger with part stock part cash payout. This i...
items.description_html:
I think this is genuinely strange as a question to ask. Gamestop is buying EBay for $55bn - but it's a merger with part stock part cash payout. This is a very common occurance and it's strange the anchors were using some weird math of counting current market cap of Gamestop as if it would be given as part of the deal.

Gamestock issues about $28bn of fresh stock. It has $28bn of cash plus debt. A shareholder of ebay gets Gamestock shares plus cash, equivalent to the amount per share that Gamestop is buying ebay for.

Gamestop's current market cap is $11bn. It will therefore become - if you want to "math" it - $11bn+$28bn fresh issue = $38bn. And for that, what matters is the effective diluted EPS of the merged entity, which is in the GME presentation.

I know he's gruff. But he's right in saying he doesn't understand the question. It's like a trick question.


Mangalam Maloo: I feel you, @andrewrsorkin! I do!

This is the funniest interview I’ve seen in my life.

There have been times, we’ve asked tough questions and managements have been evasive, but none as blatantly nonchalant.

items.description_html:
India sanctioned 20.33 crore smart meters under RDSS.

RDSS-installed so far: 4.69 crore. Nationally across all schemes: 6.13–6.55 crore.

~15.6 crore still in the pipeline under RDSS alone. ₹3.04 lakh crore scheme. Deadline extended to March 2028.

Every state that faced blackouts last week is asking: why don't we have real-time grid visibility?

Smart meters answer that. The capex is already sanctioned. The demand signal just became impossible to ignore.

items.title: Interesting - Every 2nd person who bought a Maruti Suzuki India car was a first-time car Buyer! In Q4FY26, First-time buyer was about 51%, replacement...
items.description_html:
Interesting - Every 2nd person who bought a Maruti Suzuki India car was a first-time car Buyer!

In Q4FY26, First-time buyer was about 51%, replacement buyers were 18%, and additional car buyers were 31%!

H1FY26 - 42% were first-time buyers, Q3FY26 - 48%, and Q4FY26 - 51%.

It is a very clear signal that the government's intended support for the first-time buyer is showing results!

FY26 - Maruti recorded its highest-ever annual sales of 24.2 lakh vehicles, which includes the highest-ever export of 447,000 vehicles.

Happy to see that almost 11-12L families bought their first car :)

no stock reco

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: MS on PNB UW, TP Rs 88 Core revenues were 5% below MSe. Core PPOP adjusted for reversal of retirement provisions (due to higher bond y...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
MS on PNB
UW, TP Rs 88
Core revenues were 5% below MSe.
Core PPOP adjusted for reversal of retirement provisions (due to higher bond yields) also missed MSe
Reversal of retirement provisions, lower credit costs (owing to certain lumpy reversals) & bad loan recoveries drove a strong headline PAT beat.

CLSA on PNB
O-P, TP Rs 135
PNB reported an optical PAT beat of 16% driven by lower-than-expected Opex and credit costs, despite a 5% miss on total income
Key disappointment was NII, which declined 3% QoQ despite growth in its loan book.
Loan growth of 13.7% YoY was slightly below our estimate of 15% YoY.
Deposit growth of 9% YoY was in line with last quarter, but clearly a slowdown from FY25
CASA ratio of 36% was steady which is a decent outcome.
Asset quality performance was decent with the gross slippage ratio declining 20bps YoY to 0.9%
Cut FY27-28CL PAT estimates by 3% on account of lower NII

Jefferies on PNB
Buy, TP Rs 130
Q4 profit of Rs52bn (+14% YoY) was ahead of est, as reversal of retirement benefits & write-back of provisions compensated for weaker NII.
Loan growth improved to 14% YoY, but NIM compression dragged NII.
Headroom in LDR allows PNB to grow loans despite soft deposit growth of 9%.
Slippages fell YoY & core credit costs are controlled.
Ability to sustain ROA despite transition to ECL will be key; valuations reasonable at 0.8x PB.
@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh

items.title: RT menaka doshi: Is the Tamil Nadu Model at risk? For over four decades Tamil Nadu has enjoyed what’s been popularly referred to as “bipartisan cons...
items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
Is the Tamil Nadu Model at risk?

For over four decades Tamil Nadu has enjoyed what’s been popularly referred to as “bipartisan consensus.”

As power alternated between the two main regional parties, DMK and AIADMK, one thing remained broadly constant — the drive to industrialize.

Now, the election of a newbie chief minister casts a shadow on this implicit arrangement.

Will Vijay build a tri-partisan consensus?

Click here for my latest #IndiaEdition 👇

https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-05-05/vijay-s-tamil-nadu-win-may-benefit-chandrababu-naidu-s-andhra-pradesh?srnd=undefined

items.title: Funny to see many people debating PMS vs MF Both PMS and MF have funds that have not beaten the Index and many PMS have infact beaten the Index and Al...
items.description_html:
Funny to see many people debating PMS vs MF
Both PMS and MF have funds that have not beaten the Index and many PMS have infact beaten the Index and All mutual funds over years!

Please understand - Anti PMS narrative is a win for MF mainly because a PMS takes HNI AUM away from MF!

People need to understand that the extra fees is paid to PMS for professional management and personalization.

You can't call an MF fund manager and ask him market take, but you surely can ask a PMS fund manager.

Both have their pros and cons, but the main thing is keeping the user on his wealth growth Path.

Have invested in both Mutual funds and PMS, I know the difference between the two and truly appreciate both. Underperformance in MF or PMS or stocks anyway should not be tolerated

Mahindra & Mahindra – Q4 FY26 summary

Strong earnings across Auto, Farm, Mahindra Finance and the growth businesses, with ROE at 20% and management calling FY26 a year of continued strong execution.

Business-wise breakdown

Auto: FY26 PAT grew 33% YoY to ₹7,842 crore. SUV volume grew 19% YoY to 1,118k, SUV revenue market share rose to 25.3% (+260 bps YoY), and LCV market share improved to 52.3%. Auto business margin improved to 10.9% in Q4 FY26. 100% PLI compliant

Farm: FY26 PAT grew 13% YoY to ₹4,298 crore. Domestic tractor volumes grew 24% YoY to 526k, market share improved to 43.6% (+30 bps YoY), and farm standalone PBIT margin rose to 19.8% in FY26.

Mahindra Finance: PAT grew 60% YoY to ₹2,855 crore; AUM grew 12% YoY to ₹1,34,000 crore, and GS3 improved to 3.41%. 99% secured portfolio. Udaan-important tech-platform for disbursals.

Tech Mahindra: PAT grew 14% YoY to ₹4,811 crore. TCV was $3,794 million and EBIT margin improved to 12.6% (+290 bps YoY), showing margin-led recovery.

Growth gems: Strong momentum in real estate, logistics, aerostructures, last mile mobility, hospitality and trucks & buses. 7x PAT move in Lifespaces and improving scale in logistics / EV / aero.

Key guidance & management commentary

Management is guiding for mid-single-digit industry growth in Auto & Farm, but expects Mahindra to do better because of product strength, market share gains and capacity additions. 15–18% SUV growth is possible if capacity ramps as planned.

Auto margins are expected to stay healthy, though the mix includes eSUV contract manufacturing which dilutes reported margin. Auto standalone margin was 10.4% for FY26. 10+ new launches in SUV and LCV by FY31

In Farm, the big focus is the new tractor range and product refreshes. (with transmission & power). Exited Sampo, Erkunt business units.

AI is now being treated as a real operating lever, with management citing ₹4,100 crore+ revenue potential in FY27 from AI-led adoption, alongside shorter time-to-market (10% reduced).

The strongest combination is market-share gains + margin expansion + high ROE. Auto is still the key earnings engine, Farm is stable with good margins, Finance is scaling with improving asset quality, and TechM is adding margin-led support.

Auto growth is backed by SUV leadership and EV traction, going to be less supply and more demand. No impact because of fuel prices fluctuations.
Farm is backed by new product cycles,
financial services improving with cleaner asset quality and scale.

items.title: RT Chandra R. Srikanth: 🚨 Exclusive: Cognizant may cut 12,000-15,000 jobs globally; bulk of cuts in India IT services major Cognizant is weighing a...
items.description_html:
RT Chandra R. Srikanth
🚨 Exclusive: Cognizant may cut 12,000-15,000 jobs globally; bulk of cuts in India

IT services major Cognizant is weighing a reduction in workforce that could impact 12,000-15,000 employees worldwide, with the bulk of the layoffs expected in India, multiple sources told Moneycontrol.

On April 29, the Nasdaq-listed Cognizant said it expects to incur between $230 million and $320 million in severance costs under its newly announced Project Leap, without disclosing the number of potential job losses.

The company employs more than 357,000 people, with most, over 250,000, based in India.

The layoffs estimate is based on back of the envelope calculations using typical salary and severance assumptions across geographies.

In India, the average annual salary is assumed to be around Rs 15 lakh.

With severance ranging between four to six months, the cost per employee rises to roughly $50,000, meaning fewer employees can be impacted for the same level of provisioning.

Combining these assumptions across regions yields a global estimate in the range of 12,000 to 15,000 employees.

Sources said these calculations are indicative and based on broad assumptions around salary, tenure, and geographic mix, and the final number could vary depending on how the restructuring is executed.

With @shaw_reshab

https://www.moneycontrol.com/news/business/information-technology/cognizant-may-cut-12000-15000-jobs-globally-bulk-of-cuts-in-india-13908373.html

Not as a delivery lead.

Not as a transformation architect.

items.title: RT Normal Guy: India reserves sit at $698+ billion but that number hides a lot. Gold is $131 billion of that, you cannot sell gold fast to pay for cru...
items.description_html:
RT Normal Guy
India reserves sit at $698+ billion but that number hides a lot.

Gold is $131 billion of that, you cannot sell gold fast to pay for crude imports. RBI forward book is now $104 billion in net short dollar positions, a record.

Strip both out and usable foreign currency drops to around $449 billion. That is roughly where India was in 2021.

The Iran war did not create a reserves problem. It exposed that the real liquid buffer was much thinner than the top-line number

FCNR redux sounds reasonable but the maths is completely different from 2013.

Back then US rates were near zero, so RBI could offer concessional swaps cheaply and still pull in $26 billion.

Today the Fed funds rate is 3.5-3.75%, making every hedged dollar far more expensive for RBI to absorb.
FCNR-B flows have already collapsed 87% this year to under $1 billion.

Even if RBI reopens the window, the cost per dollar raised will eat into its own balance sheet.

Import cover as a metric assumes exports go to zero. The net BoP deficit of $30 billion against $698 billion in reserves gives you a 5% drawdown rate per year, which is manageable in isolation.

The problem is three things hitting at once - crude above $110, FPI outflows crossing $20 billion in 2026, and the rupee already down 5.5% year-to-date.

Each one drains dollars through a different channel simultaneously.


Ira Dugal: India’s central bank is studying ways to boost dollar flows, including the now-famed FCNR route.

While headline level of FX reserves looks adequate, adjusted for gold and forward book, they provide about 7-odd months of import cover.

The thinking is on. Reuters @kalrajs23 and

items.title: Commodity costs were rising sharply, but Ather still improved margins. EBITDA moved from -23.3% to -2.5% over two years (~2080 bps YoY improvement), e...
items.description_html:
Commodity costs were rising sharply, but Ather still improved margins.

EBITDA moved from -23.3% to -2.5% over two years (~2080 bps YoY improvement), even as:
– Aluminium & metals prices rose ~41%
– Battery and electronics costs remained volatile

What helped:
• Better sourcing & inventory planning
• Use of alternate materials (light rare-earth magnets)
• Shift to LFP batteries
• Product design changes

A good example of how execution can offset cost pressures.

Most HNI portfolios have the equity part figured out. What they're missing is the sleeve that keeps them invested when equity does what equity does: drops 25-30% and tests every investor's nerve.

If you're 100% in equities and have never thought about what happens to your goals when a bad market shows up at exactly the wrong time, this one's worth your hour.

items.title: Atlassian's earnings calls is a great read. While Microsoft moved from per seat to usage based pricing, which is very logical, but JIRA for e.g., make...
items.description_html:
Atlassian's earnings calls is a great read. While Microsoft moved from per seat to usage based pricing, which is very logical, but JIRA for e.g., makes zero sense to move away from seat based pricing. In this case, at least, the seat compression is not visible.

Atlassian isn't reducing tokens by being clever with prompts. They're reducing tokens because with Jira/Confluence they & their clients have spent 20 years capturing structured relationships between work items, people, code, and knowledge. When Rovo (or any agent) needs context, it does a graph lookup, instead of a vector dump. This reduces dependance on token hungry RAG that enterprises use.

SAP has its own Knowledge Graph. In an enterprise, that's the moat AI cannot replace.
